questions.stem: A 58-year-old man has 8 months of heartburn and sour regurgitation, worse after large meals and lying down, with recent weight gain and nocturnal symptoms. No dysphagia, vomiting, bleeding, or weight loss. BMI 32. Most appropriate next step?
options: Clinical diagnosis of GERD with lifestyle measures and a PPI
questions.explain: Typical heartburn/regurgitation with no alarm features is a clinical GERD diagnosis → lifestyle (weight loss, head-of-bed elevation, avoid late meals) + PPI. Endoscopy is reserved for alarm features or Barrett screening.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman has 2 months of new epigastric pain, early satiety, fatigue, and 6 kg unintentional weight loss. CBC shows microcytic anemia; no NSAID use; her brother died of gastric cancer. Which feature most changes management away from empiric treatment?
options: Family history, weight loss, and iron-deficiency anemia
questions.explain: Alarm features — weight loss, iron-deficiency anemia, older age with new symptoms, and a family history of upper-GI malignancy — warrant endoscopy rather than empiric dyspepsia therapy.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man on ibuprofen TID for 4 months has black tarry stools and lightheadedness. BP 92/60, HR 118, epigastric tenderness, Hb 86 g/L. Best initial priority?
options: Resuscitation (ABCs, IV access, fluids/blood), IV PPI, and urgent endoscopy
questions.explain: This is an unstable upper GI bleed (likely NSAID ulcer). Priority is hemodynamic resuscitation first, then IV PPI and urgent endoscopy. Outpatient testing or iron alone would be unsafe.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old develops food poisoning with 12 hours of forceful vomiting, initially non-bloody, then a small amount of bright red blood. He is stable, with no liver disease or NSAID use. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explain: Hematemesis after repeated forceful vomiting is the classic Mallory-Weiss tear (a mucosal laceration at the gastroesophageal junction); most are self-limited.

questions.stem: A 61-year-old with long-standing type 2 diabetes and neuropathy has 6 months of nausea, vomiting of undigested food, early satiety, and post-prandial fullness. Endoscopy is normal; a gastric emptying study shows delayed emptying. QTc normal. Best chronic prokinetic?
questions.explain: Diabetic gastroparesis. Domperidone (a peripheral D2 antagonist that doesn't cross the blood-brain barrier) is a common chronic prokinetic with less CNS toxicity than metoclopramide — monitor QTc.

questions.stem: A 36-year-old woman with chemotherapy-induced nausea is prescribed ondansetron. Which receptor is its main target?
questions.explain: Ondansetron is a 5-HT3 receptor antagonist — 5-HT3 signaling is central to chemotherapy-induced and post-operative nausea/vomiting.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man has dysphagia that began with solids and now includes soft foods and liquids, with 9 kg weight loss, iron-deficiency anemia, and a 40-pack-year smoking history. Which diagnosis must be urgently excluded?
questions.explain: Progressive dysphagia (solids → liquids) with weight loss, anemia, and smoking are red flags for esophageal cancer — urgent endoscopy.

questions.stem: A 49-year-old woman has recurrent epigastric burning and post-prandial fullness with no alarm features and a previously normal endoscopy. Which statement best fits functional dyspepsia?
options: Symptoms are fabricated because testing is normal
Chronic gastroduodenal symptoms can arise from altered motility, visceral hypersensitivity, or brain-gut dysfunction despite normal routine testing
The diagnosis requires an abnormal CT
It is the same condition as GERD
questions.explain: Functional dyspepsia is a real disorder with normal routine testing; mechanisms include altered motility, visceral hypersensitivity, brain-gut dysfunction, microbiome and immune changes. Treat by phenotype.

questions.stem: Which parietal-cell structure is the final common pathway of gastric acid secretion and the target of the most effective acid-suppressing drugs?
questions.explain: The H+/K+-ATPase (proton pump) is the final step. PPIs irreversibly inhibit it, giving the most complete acid suppression; H2 blockers only cut the histamine input.

questions.stem: A patient finishes H. pylori eradication and needs confirmation of cure. They are still taking a PPI for symptoms. What is the most appropriate approach?
options: Urea breath test now while on the PPI
Hold the PPI for ~2 weeks, then urea breath or stool antigen test
questions.explain: PPIs cause false-negative active-infection tests. Hold the PPI (and avoid recent antibiotics/bismuth) before a urea breath or stool antigen test of cure. Serology only shows past exposure.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old needs long-term NSAIDs for arthritis but has a prior peptic ulcer. Which strategy best reduces NSAID ulcer risk if NSAIDs must continue?
options: Add a PPI (or misoprostol) for gastroprotection
Switch to a higher NSAID dose
Add an antacid only as needed
Take the NSAID on an empty stomach
questions.explain: High-risk NSAID users need gastroprotection: a PPI (or misoprostol, a prostaglandin analogue that replaces what NSAIDs suppress). A COX-2 selective agent reduces GI risk but raises cardiovascular risk.

questions.stem: A patient with known cirrhosis presents with large-volume hematemesis and hypotension. After resuscitation and IV PPI, which additions are most appropriate before/at endoscopy?
options: High-dose NSAID and tranexamic acid only
questions.explain: Suspected variceal bleeding in cirrhosis → add octreotide (splanchnic vasoconstriction) and prophylactic antibiotics (reduce infection and mortality), alongside resuscitation and urgent endoscopy (band ligation).

questions.stem: A 40-year-old has difficulty swallowing both solids and liquids from the onset of symptoms, with occasional regurgitation of undigested food. There is no weight loss. Which category is most likely?
questions.explain: Solids and liquids from onset points to an esophageal motility disorder such as achalasia (confirmed by manometry), in contrast to the solids-then-liquids progression of mechanical obstruction.

questions.stem: A patient with 10 years of poorly controlled reflux develops slowly progressive solid-food dysphagia. Endoscopy shows a smooth narrowing in the distal esophagus. What is the most likely complication?
questions.explain: Chronic acid injury can scar the distal esophagus into a peptic stricture → progressive solid-food dysphagia. (Barrett esophagus and adenocarcinoma are the other key reflux complications to keep in mind.)

questions.stem: Atrophy or autoimmune loss of which gastric cell most directly impairs vitamin B12 absorption?
questions.explain: Parietal cells secrete intrinsic factor (needed for ileal B12 absorption) as well as acid. Their loss (e.g. autoimmune gastritis) causes B12 deficiency.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old has nausea and vertigo from motion sickness on a boat. Which antiemetic class best targets this vestibular cause?
questions.explain: Vestibular nausea responds to H1 antihistamines (dimenhydrinate) and anticholinergics (scopolamine). 5-HT3 and NK1 antagonists are for chemo/post-op nausea.

questions.stem: A young woman given IV metoclopramide for migraine-associated vomiting develops an acute dystonic reaction (involuntary neck and eye muscle spasm). What explains this?
options: It crosses the blood-brain barrier and blocks central D2 receptors
questions.explain: Metoclopramide crosses the blood-brain barrier and central D2 blockade can cause acute dystonia and tardive dyskinesia — unlike domperidone, which does not cross the BBB.

questions.stem: Chronic H. pylori infection is most associated with which gastric malignancy in addition to adenocarcinoma?
questions.explain: H. pylori drives chronic active gastritis and is the classic cause of gastric MALT lymphoma (which can regress with eradication) as well as adenocarcinoma.

questions.stem: A patient on long-term high-dose PPI is reviewed. Which is the most appropriate management principle?
options: Use the lowest effective dose and periodically reassess the ongoing indication
questions.explain: PPIs are effective and often necessary, but long-term use has associations (C. difficile, enteric infection, mineral deficiencies, bone concerns). Best practice is the lowest effective dose with periodic reassessment, balanced against the indication.

questions.stem: A man who was eating steak suddenly cannot swallow and is drooling, unable to manage his own saliva. What is the appropriate disposition?
options: Expedited endoscopy for likely food-bolus obstruction
questions.explain: Inability to handle secretions indicates near-complete obstruction (food bolus/foreign body) — a GI emergency requiring expedited endoscopy. A barium study could worsen aspiration risk and isn't first.

cards.prompt: Match the gastric cells to their secretions (parietal, chief, G, ECL, D).
cards.answer: Parietal — acid + intrinsic factor; chief — pepsinogen/lipase; G — gastrin (stimulates acid); ECL — histamine (stimulates parietal via H2); D — somatostatin (inhibits acid).

cards.prompt: What three signals stimulate parietal-cell acid secretion, and what is the final pathway?
cards.answer: Gastrin, histamine, and acetylcholine; the final common pathway is the H+/K+-ATPase proton pump.

cards.prompt: How do NSAIDs cause ulcers?
cards.answer: They inhibit COX → less prostaglandin → reduced mucus, bicarbonate, mucosal blood flow, and epithelial repair, weakening defense against acid/pepsin.

cards.prompt: When is GERD a clinical diagnosis, and when is endoscopy indicated?
cards.answer: Clinical when typical heartburn/regurgitation occur without alarm features. Endoscopy for alarm features (dysphagia, vomiting, weight loss, bleeding, anemia); 24-h pH study confirms when objective proof is needed.

cards.prompt: What is Barrett esophagus and why does it matter?
cards.answer: Intestinal metaplasia of the distal esophagus from chronic reflux; it carries an increased risk of esophageal adenocarcinoma and may warrant surveillance.

cards.prompt: Rank antacids, H2 blockers, and PPIs by potency and onset.
cards.answer: Potency: PPI > H2 blocker > antacid. Onset: antacid > H2 blocker > PPI. PPIs irreversibly block the proton pump; antacids just neutralize existing acid.

cards.prompt: When should a PPI be taken for best effect?
cards.answer: 30–60 minutes before the first meal — PPIs bind actively secreting proton pumps, which are switched on by the meal.

cards.prompt: List alarm features in dyspepsia that warrant endoscopy.
cards.answer: Weight loss, progressive dysphagia, odynophagia, persistent vomiting, GI bleeding/iron-deficiency anemia, jaundice, palpable mass, family history of upper-GI cancer, and age ≥60 with new symptoms.

cards.prompt: What mechanisms underlie functional dyspepsia despite normal tests?
cards.answer: Altered motility, visceral hypersensitivity, brain-gut dysfunction, microbiome changes, immune dysregulation, and psychological stress — it is a real disorder, not fabricated.

cards.prompt: What are the two most important causes of peptic ulcer disease?
cards.answer: H. pylori infection and NSAID/ASA use.

cards.prompt: Which H. pylori tests confirm active infection / cure, and what interferes?
cards.answer: Urea breath test and stool antigen (active infection; good for test-of-cure). PPIs, recent antibiotics, and bismuth cause false negatives — hold them. Serology only shows exposure.

cards.prompt: What is the first priority in an upper GI bleed?
cards.answer: Hemodynamic resuscitation — ABCs, two large-bore IVs, fluids/blood, labs/crossmatch — before pharmacotherapy (IV PPI) and endoscopy.

cards.prompt: What do you add when variceal bleeding is suspected?
cards.answer: Octreotide (splanchnic vasoconstriction) and prophylactic antibiotics (e.g. ceftriaxone), plus urgent endoscopic band ligation, alongside resuscitation and IV PPI.

cards.prompt: How does the solids/liquids pattern localize dysphagia?
cards.answer: Solids only → mechanical; progressive solids then liquids → cancer/stricture; solids and liquids from onset → motility disorder (achalasia); intermittent solids → Schatzki ring/eosinophilic esophagitis.

cards.prompt: Why can blood-borne drugs and toxins trigger vomiting?
cards.answer: The chemoreceptor trigger zone (area postrema) lies outside the blood-brain barrier, so it senses blood-borne emetogens (drugs, uremia, hypercalcemia, toxins) and signals the medullary vomiting center.

cards.prompt: Define gastroparesis and how it is diagnosed.
cards.answer: Delayed gastric emptying without mechanical obstruction (nausea, vomiting of undigested food, early satiety). Diagnose by excluding obstruction (endoscopy ± CT) and a gastric emptying study.

cards.prompt: The final common pathway of gastric acid secretion is:
Proton pump inhibitors act by:
Which symptom in a refluxer mandates endoscopy?
Hematemesis after repeated retching/vomiting suggests:
Ondansetron's target receptor is:
Best antiemetic class for motion-sickness (vestibular) nausea:
The most common identifiable cause of gastroparesis is:
Which test CANNOT confirm H. pylori eradication?
Which prostaglandin analogue prevents NSAID-induced ulcers?
Select evidence-based GERD lifestyle measures:
Select dyspepsia alarm features warranting endoscopy:
Select common causes of upper GI bleeding:
Select conditions associated with H. pylori:
Select physiologic roles of gastric acid:

cards.text: Parietal cells secrete {{acid}} and {{intrinsic factor}}; chief cells secrete {{pepsinogen}}.
cards.explain: Intrinsic factor is needed for ileal B12 absorption.

cards.text: Upper GI bleeding arises proximal to the ligament of {{Treitz}} and presents with {{melena}} and/or {{hematemesis}}.
cards.explain: The ligament of Treitz marks the duodenojejunal junction.

cards.text: Dysphagia progressing from {{solids}} to {{liquids}} is cancer or stricture until proven otherwise.
cards.explain: Solids-and-liquids from onset instead suggests a motility disorder.

cards.text: {{PPIs}} cause false-negative active H. pylori tests, so hold them before {{urea breath}} or stool antigen testing.
cards.explain: Serology can't distinguish past from current infection.

cards.text: The best confirmatory test for GERD when objective proof is needed is a 24-hour esophageal {{pH}} study.
cards.explain: Used for unclear diagnosis or pre-operative assessment.

cards.prompt: Order the management of an unstable upper GI bleed:
items: IR/angiography or surgery if endoscopy fails

cards.prompt: Order the approach to new dyspepsia:
items: If alarms or age ≥60 with new symptoms → endoscopy
If none → H. pylori test-and-treat and/or PPI trial

cards.prompt: Order H. pylori ulcer management:
items: Confirm cure with urea breath/stool antigen off PPI

cards.prompt: Match the acid drug to its mechanism:
Match the antiemetic to its receptor:
Match the UGIB cause to its clue:
Match the prokinetic to its key feature:
Why is famotidine preferred over cimetidine?
cards.answer: Cimetidine inhibits CYP450 (many drug interactions) and can cause gynecomastia/antiandrogen effects; famotidine lacks these.

cards.prompt: What does odynophagia suggest?
cards.answer: Painful swallowing — infectious esophagitis (Candida, HSV), pill-induced injury, severe reflux, or radiation; distinct from the mechanical sticking of dysphagia.

cards.prompt: How does sucralfate protect an ulcer?
cards.answer: It forms a viscous protective barrier over the ulcer base (a mucosal protectant); it can bind other drugs, so separate dosing times.

cards.prompt: What is the trade-off with COX-2 selective NSAIDs?
cards.answer: They reduce GI ulcer/bleeding risk but increase cardiovascular/thrombotic risk — choose based on the patient's GI vs cardiovascular risk profile.

cards.prompt: What can mimic melena and must be distinguished from a true bleed?
cards.answer: Iron tablets, bismuth, and dark green stool can darken stool — history and clinical confirmation (and a digital rectal exam) help distinguish from genuine melena.

cards.prompt: What dietary changes help gastroparesis?
cards.answer: Small, frequent, low-fat, low-roughage meals; liquid enteral nutrition if solids aren't tolerated; plus antiemetics and prokinetics for symptoms.

cards.prompt: Which esophageal cancer is linked to chronic GERD/Barrett vs to smoking and alcohol?
cards.answer: Adenocarcinoma (distal) arises from chronic reflux/Barrett esophagus; squamous cell carcinoma is more associated with smoking and alcohol.

cards.prompt: How should you frame functional dyspepsia to a patient with normal tests?
cards.answer: Explain it is a real, benign disorder of gut-brain function (not 'all in their head'), and treat by phenotype — this validation itself improves outcomes.

guides.title: Week 29 · 1 Upper GI Physiology & Gastric Secretion
guides.html:
Foundations

Upper GI Physiology & Gastric Secretion

Week 29 · GI I — Upper GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The gastric cells and what they secrete, how acid is made and controlled, and the mucosal defenses NSAIDs dismantle.

Stomach regions & job

The stomach is a reservoir that mixes food into chyme, meters delivery to the duodenum, and sterilizes (pH ~2). Fundus = receptive relaxation/accommodation; corpus/antrum = trituration/mixing; pylorus = regulates emptying (solids reduced to <1–2 mm).

The cells

CellSecretesRelevance
ParietalHCl, intrinsic factorAcid (H+/K+-ATPase); IF for B12 absorption; aids iron absorption
ChiefPepsinogen, gastric lipaseProtein digestion
G cellGastrinStimulates acid
ECL cellHistamineStimulates parietal cells via H2
D cellSomatostatinInhibits acid

Acid secretion

The parietal-cell H+/K+-ATPase (proton pump) is the final common pathway. Three stimulators converge: gastrin, histamine, acetylcholine. Acid generation uses carbonic anhydrase and yields bicarbonate (the post-prandial "alkaline tide").

Why the pump matters for drugs
PPIs irreversibly block the proton pump (the final step) → the most complete acid suppression. H2 blockers cut one input (histamine). Antacids just neutralize acid already present.

Mucosal protection

Defenses: mucus (barrier), bicarbonate (neutralizes at the surface), prostaglandins (drive mucus/bicarbonate/blood flow), mucosal blood flow, and epithelial restitution.

NSAIDs break the defense
NSAIDs inhibit cyclooxygenase → less prostaglandin → less mucus, bicarbonate, blood flow, and repair → ulcers. This is why a prostaglandin analogue (misoprostol) can prevent NSAID ulcers.
Gastrin/histamine/ACh stimulate, somatostatin inhibits; the proton pump is the final step. Acid also matters for B12 (IF), iron, and killing microbes — chronic suppression has trade-offs.

guides.title: Week 29 · 2 Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD)
guides.html:
Reflux

Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD)

Week 29 · GI I — Upper GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Typical vs atypical symptoms, when it's a clinical diagnosis vs needs scoping, the complications, and the lifestyle + acid-suppression plan.

Definition & symptoms

Troublesome symptoms or complications from reflux of gastric contents. Typical: heartburn, regurgitation. Extra-esophageal: chronic cough, throat clearing, hoarseness, sore throat, globus — more likely GERD-related when typical symptoms coexist.

Diagnosis

Clinical diagnosis — unless alarm features
Typical heartburn/regurgitation with no alarm features → treat empirically (clinical diagnosis). Endoscopy if any alarm feature: dysphagia, persistent vomiting, weight loss, GI bleeding, or anemia. 24-h esophageal pH study is the best confirmatory test when objective proof is needed (unclear diagnosis, pre-operative).

Complications

ComplicationClue
EsophagitisHeartburn/pain; mucosal breaks
Peptic strictureProgressive solid-food dysphagia
Barrett esophagusIntestinal metaplasia from chronic reflux → adenocarcinoma risk
Esophageal adenocarcinomaProgressive dysphagia, weight loss, anemia, bleeding

Management

  • Lifestyle: weight loss (overweight/recent gain), avoid late-evening meals + head-of-bed elevation + left-lateral sleep (nocturnal symptoms), smoking cessation.
  • Acid suppression: PPI is most effective; H2 blocker PRN for intermittent symptoms; surgery (fundoplication) is rarely needed.
  • Long-term PPI associations discussed (C. difficile, enteric infection, mineral deficiencies, bone concerns) — use the lowest effective dose, but balance against the indication.
Typical symptoms, no red flags → lifestyle + PPI, no scope. New dysphagia, weight loss, anemia, or bleeding → endoscopy. Progressive solid-food dysphagia in a refluxer = stricture or adenocarcinoma.

guides.title: Week 29 · 3 Acid-Suppression Pharmacology
guides.html:
Acid drugs

Acid-Suppression Pharmacology

Week 29 · GI I — Upper GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Antacids, H2 blockers and PPIs — how they differ in potency and onset, and the long-term PPI trade-offs.

The three tools

ClassExamplesMechanismBest use
AntacidsCalcium carbonate, Al/Mg saltsNeutralize acid already presentRapid, short-lived relief
H2 receptor antagonistsFamotidine, cimetidine, ranitidine, nizatidineReversibly block parietal-cell H2 receptorsPRN/intermittent or breakthrough symptoms; tachyphylaxis over time
Proton pump inhibitorsOmeprazole, pantoprazole, esomeprazole, lansoprazoleIrreversibly inhibit H+/K+-ATPaseMost effective acid suppression; lowest effective dose long-term

Practical points

  • PPIs work best taken 30–60 min before the first meal (they need active pumps to bind).
  • Cimetidine inhibits CYP450 (drug interactions) and can cause gynecomastia — famotidine is preferred.
  • PPIs can cause false-negative H. pylori testing — hold before urea breath / stool antigen testing.
Long-term PPI trade-offs
Associations raised in the course: C. difficile and enteric infections, mineral deficiencies (magnesium, B12, iron), and bone/osteoporosis concerns. These are associations to weigh against a clear indication, not reasons to withhold a needed PPI — use the lowest effective dose and periodically reassess.
PPI > H2 blocker > antacid for potency; antacid > H2 > PPI for speed of onset. Hold the PPI before H. pylori testing. Reassess chronic PPIs rather than continuing reflexively.

guides.title: Week 29 · 4 Dyspepsia & Functional Dyspepsia
guides.html:
Upper-gut symptoms

Dyspepsia & Functional Dyspepsia

Week 29 · GI I — Upper GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

What dyspepsia means, the alarm features that force a scope, and how functional dyspepsia is real and phenotype-treated.

Dyspepsia vs GERD

Dyspepsia = upper-GI symptoms from the gastroduodenal region: epigastric pain/burning, post-prandial fullness, early satiety. GERD is distinct — its cardinal symptoms are heartburn and regurgitation.

Alarm features → prompt endoscopy

Scope, don't just treat, when any of these
Unintentional weight loss, progressive dysphagia, odynophagia, persistent vomiting, GI bleeding / iron-deficiency anemia, jaundice, palpable mass/lymphadenopathy, family history of upper-GI malignancy, or age ≥60 with new symptoms. These shift management from empiric therapy to endoscopy.

Uninvestigated dyspepsia (no alarms)

  • Test-and-treat H. pylori in appropriate-prevalence settings, and/or a PPI trial.
  • Endoscopy if alarm features, or refractory symptoms.

Functional dyspepsia

A real functional GI disorder: chronic/recurrent symptoms without a structural lesion on routine testing. Mechanisms: altered motility, visceral hypersensitivity, brain-gut dysfunction, microbiome and immune changes, psychological stress.

Phenotype / strategyApproach
Education + reassuranceExplain it's real and benign after evaluation
Epigastric pain/burningH. pylori test/treat; PPI trial
Post-prandial fullness / early satietyProkinetic
Pain-predominant / hypersensitivityNeuromodulator (e.g. low-dose TCA)
Stress/anxiety comorbidityPsychological therapy
Normal tests don't mean fabricated symptoms — functional dyspepsia is real. Match therapy to the phenotype: PPI for burning, prokinetic for fullness, neuromodulator for pain.

guides.title: Week 29 · 5 Peptic Ulcer Disease & H. pylori
guides.html:
Ulcers

Peptic Ulcer Disease & H. pylori

Week 29 · GI I — Upper GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The two causes that matter, how H. pylori is tested and eradicated, and the principles of ulcer healing.

What an ulcer is

Mucosal injury overwhelming protection. A true ulcer reaches at least the submucosa; an erosion is more superficial. The two dominant causes: H. pylori and NSAID/ASA use.

NSAID ulcers

COX inhibition → less prostaglandin → weakened mucus/bicarbonate/blood-flow/repair. Non-selective NSAIDs hit COX-1 and COX-2; COX-2 selective agents spare the gut but raise cardiovascular/thrombotic risk. Prevent NSAID ulcers with a PPI or misoprostol in high-risk users.

H. pylori disease

Causes chronic active gastritis → gastric and duodenal ulcers, gastric MALT lymphoma, atrophy/metaplasia/dysplasia, and gastric adenocarcinoma.

TestUse
Urea breath testActive infection; good for test-of-cure
Stool antigenActive infection; good for test-of-cure
SerologyExposure only — can't confirm eradication
Rapid urease / histologyAt endoscopy
PPIs cause false negatives
PPIs (and recent antibiotics/bismuth) suppress the organism and can produce false-negative active-infection tests — hold the PPI before urea breath or stool antigen testing when safe.

Treatment principles

  • Stop NSAIDs/ASA when possible; PPI for ulcer healing.
  • Test and treat H. pylori (e.g. PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin, or bismuth quadruple therapy with metronidazole); confirm eradication with urea breath or stool antigen.
  • Biopsy/repeat endoscopy for gastric ulcers (malignancy concern) to confirm healing.
H. pylori + NSAIDs are the two causes. Confirm eradication with urea breath/stool antigen (not serology), off PPI. Gastric ulcers get biopsied and followed — they can be cancer.

guides.title: Week 29 · 6 Upper GI Bleeding
guides.html:
Bleeding

Upper GI Bleeding

Week 29 · GI I — Upper GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Recognizing the bleed, the cause list, and the resuscitate-first algorithm — plus the variceal add-ons.

Define it

Bleeding proximal to the ligament of Treitz, presenting with melena and/or hematemesis (brisk UGIB can even cause hematochezia if unstable). Mimics of melena: iron, bismuth, dark green stool — confirm.

TermMeaning
HematemesisVomiting blood (bright red or coffee-ground)
MelenaBlack tarry stool (oxidized hemoglobin)
HematocheziaRed/maroon rectal blood (brisk upper source if unstable)

Common causes

CauseClue
Peptic ulcerNSAID/ASA, H. pylori, epigastric pain, melena
VaricesCirrhosis/portal hypertension; larger bleeds
Mallory-Weiss tearHematemesis after repeated retching/vomiting
Esophagitis / malignancyGERD/odynophagia; weight loss/dysphagia/anemia
Resuscitate first
Priority #1 is hemodynamic status: ABCs, two large-bore IVs, fluids/blood as needed, labs/crossmatch. Then IV PPI (raises pH → stabilizes clot, reduces pepsin-mediated clot breakdown) and endoscopy (diagnostic, prognostic, therapeutic). If variceal bleeding is suspected (known cirrhosis): add octreotide and antibiotics (ceftriaxone). If endoscopy fails: IR/angiography or surgery.
Stabilize before you scope. PPI for all UGIB; octreotide + antibiotics when varices are suspected. Hematemesis after vomiting = Mallory-Weiss; hematemesis in a cirrhotic = varices until proven otherwise.

guides.title: Week 29 · 7 Dysphagia
guides.html:
Swallowing

Dysphagia

Week 29 · GI I — Upper GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Oropharyngeal vs esophageal, the solids-vs-liquids pattern that localizes the cause, and the red flags that mean cancer until proven otherwise.

Oropharyngeal vs esophageal

OropharyngealEsophageal
Trouble initiating the swallow; coughing/choking, nasal regurgitation, aspirationFood sticks after swallowing; retrosternal sensation
Often neuromuscular (stroke, Parkinson, myasthenia)Structural or motility

The pattern localizes the cause

PatternThink
Solids onlyMechanical obstruction
Progressive solids → liquidsCancer or progressive stricture until proven otherwise
Intermittent solidsSchatzki ring or eosinophilic esophagitis
Solids and liquids from onsetMotility disorder (e.g. achalasia)

Related symptoms

  • Odynophagia (painful swallowing): infectious (Candida, HSV), pill-induced, severe reflux, radiation.
  • Globus: lump/tightness unrelated to swallowing; GERD, anxiety, ENT.
Red flags & the emergency
Progressive dysphagia + weight loss + anemia (± smoking/alcohol) → urgently exclude esophageal cancer. Inability to handle secretions = complete obstruction (food bolus/foreign body) → a GI emergency needing expedited endoscopy.

Investigations: endoscopy (first for most), barium swallow, manometry (motility), CT for extrinsic/staging, videofluoroscopy for neuromuscular oropharyngeal dysphagia.

Solids-then-liquids progression = mechanical (cancer/stricture); solids-and-liquids from the start = motility (achalasia). Can't manage secretions = emergency scope.

guides.title: Week 29 · 8 Nausea, Vomiting & Gastroparesis
guides.html:
Emesis

Nausea, Vomiting & Gastroparesis

Week 29 · GI I — Upper GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The vomiting pathway and how it picks the antiemetic, plus diabetic gastroparesis and the prokinetics.

The vomiting pathway

The vomiting center (medulla) is the final common pathway. Inputs: the chemoreceptor trigger zone (area postrema) — outside the blood-brain barrier, sensing blood-borne emetogens (drugs, uremia, hypercalcemia, toxins); cortex; visceral afferents; raised ICP; and the vestibular system.

Match the antiemetic to the receptor

ReceptorSettingDrugs
5-HT3Chemo, post-op, GIOndansetron, granisetron
D2CTZ-mediated; prokinetic for someProchlorperazine, metoclopramide, haloperidol
NK1Chemo / post-opAprepitant
H1 / M1Vestibular (motion, vertigo)Dimenhydrinate (H1); scopolamine (M1)

Gastroparesis

Delayed gastric emptying without mechanical obstruction: nausea, vomiting of undigested food, early satiety, post-prandial fullness, upper abdominal pain. Causes: long-standing diabetes (autonomic neuropathy), idiopathic/post-infectious, post-surgical, and drugs (opioids). Diagnose by excluding obstruction (endoscopy ± CT) and a gastric emptying study.

ProkineticMechanism / caution
DomperidoneD2 antagonist; doesn't cross the BBB — common chronic choice; QT prolongation, hyperprolactinemia
MetoclopramideD2 antagonist + 5-HT4 agonist; crosses BBB → CNS effects (dystonia, tardive dyskinesia); useful IV in acute flares
ErythromycinMotilin agonist; tachyphylaxis & QT — not ideal chronically
CTZ sits outside the blood-brain barrier — that's why blood-borne toxins trigger vomiting. Pick the antiemetic by mechanism: 5-HT3 for chemo, antihistamine/anticholinergic for vestibular, D2/prokinetic for gastroparesis (watch the QT).

guides.title: Week 29 · 9 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

GI I — Upper GI

Week 29 · GI I — Upper GI
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
Unstable upper GI bleed → ABCs + IV access + fluids/blood FIRST, then IV PPI + urgent endoscopy
Suspected variceal bleed (cirrhosis + hematemesis) → add octreotide + prophylactic antibiotics
Can't handle secretions / drooling with dysphagia → complete obstruction (food bolus) — expedited endoscopy
Progressive solids→liquids dysphagia + weight loss/anemia → exclude esophageal cancer urgently
Dyspepsia with alarm features (weight loss, anemia, bleeding, age ≥60 new) → endoscopy, not empiric therapy
Metoclopramide → can cause acute dystonia (crosses BBB)

Physiology Physiology

Parietal cell acid + intrinsic factor
Chief / G / ECL / D pepsinogen / gastrin / histamine / somatostatin
Acid stimulators gastrin, histamine, ACh
Final pathway H+/K+-ATPase proton pump
Defense mucus, bicarbonate, prostaglandins, blood flow
NSAIDs ↓prostaglandin → ulcers

GERD GERD

Typical heartburn + regurgitation
Clinical dx if no alarm features
Endoscopy alarm features (dysphagia, bleed, weight loss)
Confirm test 24-h esophageal pH study
Complications esophagitis, stricture, Barrett, adenocarcinoma
Treat lifestyle + PPI

Acid drugs Acid Rx

Potency PPI > H2 blocker > antacid
Onset antacid > H2 > PPI
PPI timing 30–60 min before first meal
Cimetidine CYP450 inhibition, gynecomastia
Long-term PPI lowest effective dose; reassess

Dyspepsia / PUD Dyspepsia

Alarm features weight loss, anemia, bleed, dysphagia, age ≥60
No alarms H. pylori test-and-treat / PPI trial
Functional dyspepsia real; treat by phenotype
PUD causes H. pylori + NSAID/ASA
Test of cure urea breath/stool antigen, OFF PPI
NSAID prevention PPI or misoprostol

UGIB UGIB

Define proximal to ligament of Treitz; melena/hematemesis
Priority resuscitate first
All UGIB IV PPI + endoscopy
Variceal octreotide + antibiotics
Mallory-Weiss hematemesis after retching

Dysphagia Dysphagia

Oropharyngeal trouble initiating; cough/choke/nasal regurg
Esophageal food sticks after swallowing
Solids→liquids mechanical (cancer/stricture)
Solids+liquids onset motility (achalasia)
Red flags weight loss + anemia → cancer

Nausea / gastroparesis Emesis

CTZ area postrema, outside BBB → blood-borne triggers
5-HT3 ondansetron (chemo/post-op)
Vestibular antihistamine / anticholinergic
Gastroparesis delayed emptying, no obstruction (diabetes)
Domperidone no BBB crossing; watch QT
Metoclopramide crosses BBB → dystonia
Week 29 · GI I — Upper GI · cram sheet · cue → answer

facets.mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits the H+/K+-ATPase proton pump
facets.adverse: Long-term: C. difficile/enteric infection, low Mg/B12, bone concerns
facets.monitoring: Take 30–60 min before meals; lowest effective dose; hold before H. pylori testing

facets.use: GERD/PUD; IV for upper GI bleeding

facets.adverse: Constipation, rebound acid; milk-alkali with excess
QT prolongation, taste disturbance; resistance limits use
Metallic taste, disulfiram reaction with alcohol

facets.mechanism: Prostaglandin E1 analogue — restores mucosal defense
Forms a protective barrier over the ulcer base
